currentUse: Private single-family residence at 316 Pine St (Hanover section), Nanticoke, PA 18634 — a 3-bedroom, 4-bath, ~4,300 sq ft home retaining the original stained-glass windows, hardwood floors, and bell tower
summary: The former St. Joseph's Lithuanian church building in the Hanover section of Nanticoke is standing and has been a private single-family home for two decades. The large wooden church closed in 2004 and was bought by Nanticoke residents Bernie and Toni Norieka, who sold their house to fund converting it into their home, keeping the original 9-foot stained-glass windows. Global True Lithuania documents that the stained glass, decor, some furniture, the Lithuanian inscriptions, and a Lithuanian priest memorial in the churchyard all survive, calling it arguably the most authentic Lithuanian church building among those converted to other uses; the bell tower was made into a room with valley views. The aging owners later listed the renovated home (WNEP reported they planned to stay at their Florida residence), and property records show 316 Pine St — a former church built in 1915 — last sold on May 18, 2022 for $340,000. The buyer's identity has not been published, so whether the current owner is of Lithuanian descent is unknown. Note that a different St. Joseph's in Nanticoke — the Slovak parish, closed 2010 into St. Faustina Parish — was demolished by the Diocese of Scranton in February 2019 and is frequently confused with the Lithuanian church in news coverage.

developments.headline: Church closes; Nanticoke couple buys it to convert into their home
developments.detail: After the church's 2004 closure, Bernie Norieka of Nanticoke sold his home to convert the former St. Joseph's Church in the Hanover section into a residence for himself and his wife Toni, keeping the original 9-foot stained-glass windows intact. Global True Lithuania likewise records the church 'closed in 2004' and was 'bought by a local architect Norieka who transformed it into a single-family home.'

sources.title: Building's Saving Grace: Couple Converting Church Into Place They'll Call Home
Pennsylvania Northern Coal Region Lithuanian sites

developments.headline: Different church: the Slovak St. Joseph's in Nanticoke is demolished (disambiguation)
developments.detail: The Diocese of Scranton demolished St. Joseph's Church in Nanticoke — the Slovak parish founded c. 1889, closed 2010 into St. Faustina Parish, unsold 2013–2018 — in February 2019. This is NOT the Lithuanian St. Joseph's; the two are easily conflated in search results.

sources.title: Saint Joseph's Church demolished in Nanticoke
Striking Photos Show Demolition Of Century-Old Pennsylvania Catholic Church

developments.headline: Renovated church-home listed for sale as owners relocate to Florida
developments.detail: WNEP reported the century-old former St. Joseph's Church atop the hill on Pine Street — renovated with original stained glass and wood floors, an upgraded kitchen, 3 bedrooms and 4 bathrooms — was for sale through realtor Mark Nicholson; the aging owners planned to stay at their other home in Florida. (Article publication date could not be retrieved; the WNEP page returned 403 and has no Wayback snapshot.)

sources.title: Century-old Church Renovated into Home for Sale in Nanticoke

developments.headline: Property sells for $340,000
developments.detail: Property records for 316 Pine St, Nanticoke PA 18634 — described as a 'stately former church circa 1915' transformed into a residence with original stained-glass windows, period hardwood floors, and valley views from the bell tower — show it last sold on May 18, 2022 for $340,000. The buyer has not been publicly identified.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Three refinements and one likely-outdated fact. (1) Ownership status: archive lists 'diocese_rc', but the building has been privately owned since the 2004-2005 Norieka purchase and changed hands again May 18, 2022 for $340,000 — it is no longer diocesan property. (2) 'Lithuanian-descent owner' is likely outdated: the Norieka family (Lithuanian surname, matching the archive note) sold in May 2022; the current owner's identity and heritage are unpublished. (3) 'Interior fully intact' was verified as of the Global True Lithuania documentation and the pre-2022 sale listing (stained glass, Lithuanian inscriptions, priest memorial, some furniture); its condition under the post-2022 owner is unverified. (4) Parish status refinement: Global True Lithuania states the building 'never was a separate Lithuanian parish' but was a church served by the Lithuanian priest from Wanamie (St. Mary's) — the archive's framing of it as a parish may overstate its canonical status (single source, unconfirmed). No contradiction on the core facts: closed, converted to a private residence, interior preserved — all confirmed.
gaps: Founding date of the Lithuanian congregation not found (building dated 1915 in the property record). The 2004 closure's canonical mechanics (which parish it was merged into, diocesan decree) were not found; searches of Diocese of Scranton consolidation lists cover the 2008-2012 restructuring, which postdates this closure. The WNEP for-sale article's publication date and the asking price could not be retrieved (page returns 403 to fetches; no Wayback Machine snapshot exists); its content was obtained via search-result snippets only, as was the Trulia sale record (direct page 403). The May 2022 buyer is unidentified, so current ownership heritage and current interior condition are unknown. No preservation campaigns, reunions, or press coverage of the Lithuanian church found for 2023-2026. The Global True Lithuania claim that it was never a separate parish, and the 'local architect' description of Bernie Norieka (Times Leader does not call him an architect), rest on single sources. Searches used both 'Šv. Juozapo' anglicized forms and disambiguated against the Slovak St. Joseph's (demolished 2019) and Duryea's St. Joseph's Lithuanian.
